title: Dog Ate Macadamia Nuts: Symptoms, Toxicity, and Triage Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate macadamia nuts, learn the symptoms of toxicity, urgency levels, and why you should contact a vet immediately for guidance.
content_html:
TL;DR: Macadamia nut ingestion in dogs causes weakness and vomiting; contact a veterinarian or poison control immediately to assess the situation, especially if chocolate or raisins were also consumed.

What is macadamia nut toxicity in dogs and what are the symptoms?

Macadamia nuts are highly toxic to dogs, though the exact mechanism of the toxin is still not fully understood by veterinary science. When a dog ingests these nuts, it can lead to a variety of neurological and gastrointestinal issues. Symptoms often include weakness in the hind legs, vomiting, tremors, and a high body temperature. While the condition is rarely fatal, it can be very distressing for both the pet and the owner.

Is my dog eating macadamia nuts an emergency and what should I do?

  • The urgency level for macadamia nut ingestion is classified as Medium.
  • While most dogs recover with supportive care, you should contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.
  • Medical intervention may be required if your dog is experiencing severe vomiting or profound weakness.
  • Prompt action is especially important if the nuts were coated in chocolate or mixed with raisins, as these ingredients significantly increase the danger.

Why should I provide a photo of the product packaging to help with triage?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a photo of the product packaging or the remaining nuts can be incredibly helpful for accurate assessment.
  • A photo of the ingredient list allows the medical team to check for other toxins and calculate the exact dosage of macadamia nuts your dog may have consumed.
  • This information helps the vet determine if they need to induce vomiting or if the situation can be managed with home monitoring.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of macadamia nuts by dogs can result in a nonfatal syndrome characterized by vomiting, ataxia, weakness, hyperthermia, and depression. Tremors may also be present, potentially secondary to muscle weakness. Clinical signs typically manifest within 12 hours of ingestion, with dogs showing signs after ingesting as little as 2.4 g of nuts/kg body weight. Diagnosis is based on a history of exposure and clinical signs. Differential diagnoses include ethylene glycol toxicosis, ingestion of hypotensive agents, and infectious diseases. Treatment involves inducing emesis with apomorphine or hydrogen peroxide (or gastric lavage in sedated animals), followed by administration of activated charcoal (1-4 g/kg, PO), repeated every 12 hours in symptomatic animals. Supportive care includes maintaining thermoregulation, correcting acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities, and monitoring cardiac status. Clinical signs generally resolve within 12-48 hours, although they may persist up to 72 hours in severe cases.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2967)

faqs.question: How many macadamia nuts are toxic to dogs?
faqs.answer: Toxicity can be seen with as little as 2 grams of nuts per kilogram of body weight. In many cases, just one or two nuts can cause symptoms in small to medium-sized dogs.

faqs.question: How soon will my dog show symptoms?
faqs.answer: Symptoms of macadamia nut poisoning typically develop within 3 to 12 hours after ingestion.

faqs.question: Will my dog recover from eating macadamia nuts?
faqs.answer: Yes, most dogs make a full recovery within 24 to 48 hours, although veterinary support is often needed to manage pain and dehydration during that time.
